summary: Apple announced a new USB-C Apple Pencil at $79, which makes sense as Lightning fades but highlights the growing complexity of the iPad lineup with three Pencil models supporting different iPads. Gruber argues the iPad product line has become over-complicated compared to Apple's cleaner iPhone, MacBook, and Watch lineups. He outlines a wishlist for 2024: drop the 9th-gen iPad, cut prices on the 10th-gen, bring Face ID to iPad Air, and deliver a major iPad Pro revision. He advocates for a clear three-tier structure of good/better/best that makes the buying decision simple. Gruber emphasizes that Face ID is a clearly superior technology that should be standard on all but the cheapest iPad, just as it is across the iPhone lineup. He also takes a side swipe at the 13-inch MacBook Pro as the one illogical product in Apple's otherwise clean MacBook lineup.
key_points: Three Apple Pencil models with different features and iPad compatibility exemplify the over-complicated iPad lineup
Apple needs a clear three-tier iPad strategy: regular iPad (good), iPad Air (better, best for most), iPad Pro (best with pro features)
Face ID should be standard on all iPads except the entry model, as it is with iPhones — it's too good a feature to gate behind the Pro tier
The Magic Keyboard needs a major revision to be thinner, lighter, and more durable, especially the white models which age poorly
The 13-inch MacBook Pro is the one illogical product in Apple's MacBook lineup, outsold on specs by the cheaper MacBook Air but kept alive by the 'Pro' name
money_quotes: The fact that there are now three Apple Pencil models, all with different features and which are supported by different iPads, exemplifies just how over-complicated the iPad product lineup is.
The point should be to make the question 'Which iPad should I buy?' as easy to answer as possible.
I think Apple has made that question pretty easy to answer for iPhones, MacBooks, and Watches — and for peripherals like AirPods. Now do it for iPads.
Face ID isn't just a different form of bio-authentication than Touch ID, it a clearly superior one.
Touch ID on the iPad Air makes it feel easy to unlock. Face ID on the iPad Pro makes it seems like it wasn't even locked in the first place.
With iPhones, Face ID is now in every model they sell but the SE. That's how it ought to be for iPads too: in every one of them but the price-focused entry model.
My theory is that there's a segment of the market that would be served better by the MacBook Air but who believe that they need a 'Pro' MacBook. The specs don't matter to them — it's the name.
key_insight: The iPad lineup has become needlessly complicated, and Apple needs to simplify it into a clear good/better/best tiering — the same clarity it has achieved with iPhones, MacBooks, and Watches.
